message:
fix(core): use record scratch for update payload, route delete payload via caller

Two related corrections to the prepared-direct update/delete hot
paths.

== Update path: payload_buf must use the RECORD scratch ==

The `execute_prepared_direct_simple_update` flow was binding
`payload_buf` from `self.prepared_direct_insert_cell_scratch` —
the scratch buffer reserved for B-tree CELL BYTES (the on-disk cell
header + payload + overflow pointer that `BtCursor::write_cell`
consumes). The `update_row_scratch` was being used for the parsed
column vector, which is correct, but the payload buffer that holds
the SERIALIZED RECORD bytes (header varints + body) was sharing
storage with the cell-bytes scratch.

That sharing is OK in isolation because `payload_buf` is consumed
before the cursor writes any cell bytes, but it broke as soon as
the cursor needed to retain its own cell scratch across calls into
`Connection`-state-mutating helpers (the QF consultation +
maintenance the comment a few lines up calls out). The cursor's
internal cell-scratch borrow was racing the
Connection-level borrow on the same RefCell, producing a panic on
the second `borrow_mut()` of `prepared_direct_insert_cell_scratch`.

Switch the update path to use
`self.prepared_direct_insert_record_scratch` for `payload_buf`.
That scratch is reserved for record serialization specifically,
which is what `payload_buf` actually holds. The cell scratch is
freed up for the cursor's exclusive use.

The cursor's cell-scratch ownership is now plumbed explicitly via
a `swap_cell_scratch(&mut cell_scratch)` call before AND after the
inner `execute_prepared_direct_simple_update_with_cursor(...)`
call (and the matching delete path below). The swap handoff means
the cursor borrows the Connection's cell scratch only for the
duration of its update/delete work, then returns it. No
longer-lived double-borrow window.

== Delete path: payload_buf must come from the caller ==

The matching `execute_prepared_direct_simple_delete_with_cursor`
helper was reaching into
`self.prepared_direct_insert_cell_scratch` directly, which is
both the wrong scratch (same record-vs-cell mismatch as above) AND
locks in a Connection-level borrow inside the helper that the
caller can't reason about.

Lift `payload_buf` to a parameter:
`fn execute_prepared_direct_simple_delete_with_cursor(..., payload_buf: &mut Vec<u8>, cursor: ...)`.
The caller already holds a `payload_buf` borrow on the record
scratch (matching the update path's fix above) and threads it
through. Inside the helper, `let mut payload_buf = self.prepared_direct_insert_cell_scratch.borrow_mut();`
goes away; the helper just clears and writes into the borrow it
was handed.

The caller in turn takes its own `cell_scratch` borrow and
performs the same `swap_cell_scratch(&mut cell_scratch)`
before/after the inner call, so the cursor sees its dedicated
cell-bytes scratch for the duration of the delete.

== Net effect ==

Two scratch buffers (record bytes vs. cell bytes) are now used as
they were named for. The cursor's interior cell scratch can be
swapped against the Connection's cell scratch around any update
or delete without overlapping the record-payload borrow. The
panic-on-double-borrow under contention goes away; ergonomic
shape (`payload_buf` is owned by the caller, swapped into the
cursor when needed) matches the rest of the connection-level
prepared paths.
